name: Cincinnatus
description: Lucius Quinctius Cincinnatus was a Roman citizen-soldier, statesman, and dictator of the early Roman Republic. He is celebrated as a symbol of civic virtue, humility, and selfless service to the state. Cincinnatus famously relinquished his dictatorial power and returned to his farm after leading the Roman army to victory against the Aequi in 458 BC. Called from his plow to serve as dictator, he quickly defeated the enemy and, despite the immense power vested in him, he resigned his position after only fifteen days, returning to his simple life as a farmer. This act of selflessness and dedication to the common good made him a legendary figure in Roman history and a model for future leaders. His story highlights the Roman ideals of duty, simplicity, and the importance of prioritizing the welfare of the Republic over personal ambition. He was later called upon again to quell a plot to undermine the Roman state, again proving his dedication to Rome. Cincinnatus's legacy endures as an embodiment of Roman virtue and a reminder of the importance of civic responsibility.
subtitle: The epitome of Roman civic virtue

keyMyths.title: Dictatorship and Victory
keyMyths.description: Cincinnatus was appointed dictator to lead Rome against the Aequi. He swiftly defeated them and returned to his farm only fifteen days later, refusing to abuse his power.

keyMyths.title: The Plowman's Call
keyMyths.description: Envoys found Cincinnatus plowing his field and informed him of his appointment as dictator. He left his plow without hesitation to serve Rome.

keyMyths.title: Second Appointment
keyMyths.description: At an advanced age, Cincinnatus was once again called upon to serve Rome when Spurius Maelius attempted to gain power through grain distribution.

sources.author: Livy
sources.description: A comprehensive history of Rome, including the story of Cincinnatus.
sources.text: A comprehensive history of Rome, including the story of Cincinnatus.

sources.text: References to Roman figures and customs.

associations.name: Civic Duty
associations.significance: Represents the ideal of prioritizing the state's needs above personal interests.

associations.name: Humility
associations.significance: Embodies the rejection of personal ambition and the acceptance of a simple life.

associations.name: Dictator
associations.significance: Illustrates the concept of temporary, necessary authoritarianism in times of crisis.

associations.name: Farmer
associations.significance: Highlights the connection to the land and the virtue of agricultural labor.

associations.name: Plow
associations.significance: Symbolizes his willingness to return to a life of simplicity and hard work.

associations.name: Selflessness
associations.significance: Demonstrates the act of putting the welfare of the community before individual gain.

associations.name: Leadership
associations.significance: Showcases effective leadership in times of crisis, inspiring confidence and loyalty.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like Cincinnatus, Aristides embodied civic virtue and incorruptibility, serving his state with integrity and declining personal gain.
Yu the Great, similar to Cincinnatus, was known for his dedication to public service, particularly in controlling floods, foregoing personal comfort for the greater good.
Gideon, like Cincinnatus, was called from a humble background to lead his people in battle, demonstrating humility and obedience to a higher calling.
Regulus, similar to Cincinnatus, exemplified Roman virtue through his dedication to duty, famously returning to Carthage to face certain death after advocating against prisoner exchange.

worshipPractices.name: Virtue Exemplar
worshipPractices.description: While not formally worshipped, Cincinnatus was held up as a moral exemplar in Roman society. His story was recounted to inspire citizens to emulate his virtues of duty, humility, and self-sacrifice.

cultural.modernLegacy: Cincinnatus's legacy persists as a symbol of republican virtue and civic responsibility. He is often invoked in political discourse to represent selfless leadership and the importance of prioritizing public service over personal ambition. The Society of the Cincinnati, founded by officers of the American Revolutionary War, is named in his honor.

extendedContent.title: Early Life
extendedContent.content: Lucius Quinctius Cincinnatus lived during the early Roman Republic, a period marked by constant territorial conflicts and the development of Roman civic institutions. Details of his early life are scarce, but he belonged to a patrician family, the Quinctii, suggesting he possessed a certain social standing and privilege. He was a farmer who cultivated his own land, a practice considered virtuous in early Roman society, embodying self-sufficiency and a connection to the land. Cincinnatus likely participated in Roman political life before his famous dictatorship, gaining experience in governance and military matters. The limited information about his early years contrasts sharply with the extensive accounts of his later actions, highlighting the impact of his brief but decisive leadership during a critical moment in Roman history. This juxtaposition adds to the mystique surrounding Cincinnatus, solidifying his image as an ordinary citizen who rose to extraordinary heights when duty called.

extendedContent.title: Greatest Quests
extendedContent.content: Cincinnatus's most renowned episode occurred in 458 BC. Rome was at war with the Aequi, and the Roman army was trapped and on the verge of defeat. The Senate, recognizing the gravity of the situation, appointed Cincinnatus dictator, granting him absolute authority. According to legend, messengers found him plowing his fields. He accepted the appointment, swiftly raised an army, marched to the aid of the besieged Roman forces, and decisively defeated the Aequi. After his triumph, instead of clinging to power, Cincinnatus voluntarily resigned his dictatorship just fifteen days after assuming it, returning to his farm and his simple life. This act of selfless abdication became his defining characteristic and the cornerstone of his enduring fame. A second instance of his leadership occurred in 439 BC when he was again appointed dictator to deal with Spurius Maelius, who was suspected of plotting to become king. Cincinnatus swiftly suppressed the alleged conspiracy, further cementing his reputation for decisive action and unwavering loyalty to the Republic.

extendedContent.title: Legacy
extendedContent.content: Cincinnatus's legacy extends far beyond the borders of ancient Rome. He has become an enduring symbol of civic virtue, selfless service, and republican ideals. His story has been recounted and celebrated throughout history, serving as an inspiration to leaders and citizens alike. During the American Revolution, Cincinnatus was particularly admired by figures like George Washington, who saw him as a model for responsible leadership. The Society of the Cincinnati, an organization of officers from the Continental Army, was founded in his honor, reflecting the enduring appeal of his example. Cincinnatus's actions embody the concept of a citizen-soldier who prioritizes the needs of the state above personal ambition, a principle that continues to resonate in democratic societies. His story serves as a reminder that true leadership lies not in the pursuit of power but in the willingness to serve the common good and relinquish authority when the task is complete.

shortDescription: Roman hero and statesman, Cincinnatus was the epitome of civic virtue. He famously relinquished dictatorial power to return to his farm, symbolizing selfless service.
